abstract: The biological identity and ultimate physiological fate of nanoparticles (NPs) are dictated by the "protein corona," a layer of adsorbed biomolecules that forms immediately upon contact with physiological fluids. While the influence of NP physicochemical properties on corona composition is well documented, the impact of the specific biological source remains poorly understood. In this study, we hypothesize that alterations in the human plasma proteome caused by specific diseases significantly modify the resulting NP corona. To test this, we incubated hydrophobic polystyrene and hydrophilic silica nanoparticles with plasma samples obtained from subjects across a wide spectrum of medical conditions, including various cancers, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, coagulation disorders (hemophilia and hypofibrinogenemia), and pregnancy. Using SDS-PAGE, dynamic light scattering, and zeta potential analysis, we demonstrate that the disease state of the donor serves as a critical determinant of the protein corona’s molecular signature. Our findings reveal that patients with distinct pathologies produce unique, identifiable coronas even when exposed to identical nanomaterials. Consequently, we propose the concept of the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) as a vital factor in the nanobiointerface. Integrating the PPC into the design and evaluation of nanomedicines is essential for the transition toward effective, patient-specific therapeutic and diagnostic applications.
